content.text: I want to provide support for the streaming configuration in NuShell, but the problem is that NuShell does not support it. We investigated this previously. I want to create an issue on the NuGet GitHub repository asking for support. Obviously, I would need to explain what this feature is in oh-my-posh and what type of functionality would be needed from them so that we can support it. Can you write a detailed analysis in markdown or an explanation I can share with them, with a sequence flow, etc., explaining where it touches the current new initialization script and what would need to be added on their end if you wanted to support this?

I'll research how the streaming feature works in the current shells and what the Nushell init looks like, then write the shareable analysis. Let me start by loading the project knowledge and finding the streaming implementation.

1	package prompt
2	
3	import (
4		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/config"
5		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/shell"
6	)

8	// TransientMarker prefixes a streamed record that contains the transient prompt
9	// rather than a primary prompt update. Shells cache such a record so rendering
10	// the transient prompt on Enter needs no additional CLI call.
11	const TransientMarker = "\x1e"
12	
13	// StreamPrimary returns a channel that yields prompt updates as segments complete.
14	//
15	// The engine + terminal package globals are not thread-safe, so at most one
16	// StreamPrimary producer goroutine may be rendering at any given time. Callers
17	// that need to interrupt an in-flight cycle (e.g. a long-lived server handling
18	// a new render request before the previous one finished) must call Abort and
19	// wait for it to return before starting a new cycle - Abort blocks until the
20	// producer goroutine has fully exited.
21	func (e *Engine) StreamPrimary() <-chan string {
22		// Initialize streaming infrastructure BEFORE launching goroutine
23		// This ensures the channel exists when segments start timing out
24		e.streamingResults = make(chan *config.Segment, 100)
25		e.allBlocks = e.Config.Blocks
26		e.abort = make(chan struct{})
27		e.done = make(chan struct{})
28	
29		out := make(chan string, 10)
30	
31		// sendRecord delivers a record unless the cycle gets aborted. A plain
32		// channel send could block forever once the buffer fills against a
33		// stalled consumer, keeping the producer from ever observing the abort
34		// and deadlocking Abort() - which waits for this goroutine to exit.
35		// Returns false when the cycle is aborted; the producer must stop then.
36		sendRecord := func(record string) bool {
37			select {
38			case out <- record:
39				return true
40			case <-e.abort:
41				return false
42			}
43		}
44	
45		// aborted reports whether the cycle has been asked to stop. Once true,
46		// the producer must not touch the engine's prompt builder or the
47		// terminal package globals again - those are shared with the next cycle.
48		aborted := func() bool {
49			select {
50			case <-e.abort:
51				return true
52			default:
53				return false
54			}
55		}
56	
57		// The transient prompt must render in the same goroutine as the primary
58		// updates: both write to the engine's prompt builder and the terminal
59		// package's global state.
60		sendTransient := func() {
61			if aborted() {
62				return
63			}
64	
65			// The zsh script caches a streamed transient record as PS1 only and
66			// resets RPROMPT (see _omp_zle-line-init in omp.zsh), so the record
67			// cannot carry a right-aligned template. Skip it to make the script
68			// fall back to the eval path which sets both PS1 and RPROMPT.
69			if e.Env.Shell() == shell.ZSH && e.Config.TransientPrompt != nil && len(e.Config.TransientPrompt.RightTemplate) != 0 {
70				return
71			}
72	
73			// The zsh script renders the transient prompt one column narrower to avoid
74			// a redundant blank line when a filler is configured and the input is empty
75			// (see _omp_zle-line-init in omp.zsh), mirror that for the streamed record.
76			if e.Env.Shell() == shell.ZSH {
77				e.rectifyTerminalWidth(-1)
78				defer e.rectifyTerminalWidth(1)
79			}
80	
81			sendRecord(TransientMarker + e.ExtraPrompt(Transient))
82		}
83	
84		go func() {
85			defer close(e.done)
86			defer close(out)
87			// Registered last so it runs first during unwinding: a panic in
88			// segment/render code then costs this one cycle instead of the whole
89			// process - which matters for the long-lived serve daemon. The closes
90			// above still run afterwards, so Abort() and the record consumer both
91			// observe a normally-ended cycle.
92			defer func() {
93				_ = recover()
94			}()
95	
96			if aborted() {
97				return
98			}
99	
100			// Render and send initial prompt with pending segments
101			if !sendRecord(e.Primary()) {
102				return
103			}
104	
105			sendTransient()
106	
107			if e.countPendingSegments() == 0 {
108				// No segment is executing in the background, so nothing can send
109				// on streamingResults after this point - safe to close.
110				close(e.streamingResults)
111				return
112			}
113	
114			// Listen for segment completions. A segment that timed out keeps
115			// executing in the background (trackPendingSegment) even after this
116			// loop returns; notifySegmentCompletion sends via select/default so
117			// it never blocks such a goroutine, but that also means
118			// streamingResults must NOT be closed here on the abort path - a
119			// stray late send on a closed channel would panic. Only close it once
120			// every pending segment has actually reported in (countPendingSegments
121			// reaches 0), which is the one path guaranteed to have no further
122			// senders. On abort, leave the channel open and let it be garbage
123			// collected once the last stray sender (and this Engine) is dropped.
124			for {
125				select {
126				case <-e.abort:
127					return
128				case _, ok := <-e.streamingResults:
129					if !ok {
130						return
131					}
132	
133					if aborted() {
134						continue
135					}
136	
137					if !sendRecord(e.renderFromBlocks()) {
138						return
139					}
140	
141					if e.countPendingSegments() == 0 {
142						// refresh the transient prompt now the context is fully resolved
143						sendTransient()
144						close(e.streamingResults)
145						return
146					}
147				}
148			}
149		}()
150	
151		return out
152	}
153	
154	// Abort signals the active StreamPrimary cycle (if any) to stop rendering and
155	// blocks until its producer goroutine has fully exited, so the caller can
156	// safely start a new cycle (on a new Engine) immediately after Abort returns.
157	// It is safe to call multiple times and safe to call when no cycle is active
158	// or the cycle has already finished on its own.
159	//
160	// Abort does not wait for segments still executing in the background after a
161	// per-segment timeout (see trackPendingSegment) - those belong to this
162	// Engine instance only and are expected to be abandoned along with it; they
163	// will report to a now-unread streamingResults channel (via
164	// notifySegmentCompletion's non-blocking send) until they finish on their own
165	// and get garbage collected with this Engine.
166	func (e *Engine) Abort() {
167		if e.abort == nil {
168			return
169		}
170	
171		select {
172		case <-e.abort:
173			// already aborted
174		default:
175			close(e.abort)
176		}
177	
178		if e.done != nil {
179			<-e.done
180		}
181	}
182	
183	// countPendingSegments counts how many segments are marked as pending
184	func (e *Engine) countPendingSegments() int {
185		count := 0
186		e.pendingSegments.Range(func(_, _ any) bool {
187			count++
188			return true
189		})
190		return count
191	}
192	
193	// renderFromBlocks re-renders the complete prompt using stored block data
194	func (e *Engine) renderFromBlocks() string {
195		// Reset prompt builder
196		e.prompt.Reset()
197		e.currentLineLength = 0
198		e.activeSegment = nil
199		e.previousActiveSegment = nil
200		e.rprompt = ""
201		e.rpromptLength = 0
202	
203		return e.primaryInternal(true)
204	}
205	
206	// trackPendingSegment continues execution for a timed-out segment in the background
207	func (e *Engine) trackPendingSegment(segment *config.Segment, done chan bool) {
208		if e.streamingResults == nil {
209			return
210		}
211	
212		// Segment is already pre-registered in pendingSegments map
213		go func() {
214			<-done
215			segment.Pending = false
216			e.notifySegmentCompletion(segment)
217		}()
218	}
219	
220	// notifySegmentCompletion sends completed segment to the streaming results channel
221	func (e *Engine) notifySegmentCompletion(segment *config.Segment) {
222		if e.streamingResults == nil {
223			return
224		}
225	
226		if _, ok := e.pendingSegments.LoadAndDelete(segment.Name()); ok {
227			select {
228			case e.streamingResults <- segment:
229				// Successfully notified consumer
230			default:
231				// Consumer not ready or already exited
232				// This can happen if segment completes after consumer finishes
233			}
234		}
235	}

311	# JSON string escaping with builtins. Joining the result with '' drops any

315	function _omp_serve_escape

372	# poll the tempfile until it holds this cycle's primary record
373	function _omp_serve_read_primary
374	    set --local us (printf '\x1f')

376	    set --local elapsed 0
377	
378	    while test $elapsed -lt $timeout
379	        if test -s "$_omp_serve_tempfile"
380	            set --local record (cat "$_omp_serve_tempfile" | string collect)
381	            set --local id (string split --max 1 -- $us $record)[1]
382	            if test "$id" = "$_omp_serve_cycle"
383	                string replace -- "$id$us" '' $record | string collect
384	                return 0
385	            end
386	        end
387	        sleep 0.01
388	        set elapsed (math $elapsed + 10)

391	    return 1

399	        if not _omp_serve_start

Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\writing-clearly-and-concisely

# Writing Clearly and Concisely

## Overview

Write with clarity and force. This skill covers what to do (Strunk) and what not to do (AI patterns).

## When to Use This Skill

Use this skill whenever you write content a human will read:

- Emails, messages, and any direct communication
- Documents, reports, summaries, proposals
- Documentation, README files, technical explanations
- Commit messages, pull request descriptions
- Error messages, UI copy, help text, comments
- Any other prose meant for human eyes

**If a human will read it, this skill applies — no exceptions.**

## Limited Context Strategy

When context is tight:

1. Write your draft using judgment
2. Dispatch a subagent with your draft and the relevant section file
3. Have the subagent copyedit and return the revision

Loading a single section (~1,000-4,500 tokens) instead of everything saves significant context.

## Elements of Style

William Strunk Jr.'s *The Elements of Style* (1918) teaches you to write clearly and cut ruthlessly.

### Rules

**Elementary Rules of Usage (Grammar/Punctuation)**:

1. Form possessive singular by adding 's
2. Use comma after each term in series except last
3. Enclose parenthetic expressions between commas
4. Comma before conjunction introducing co-ordinate clause
5. Don't join independent clauses by comma
6. Don't break sentences in two
7. Participial phrase at beginning refers to grammatical subject

**Elementary Principles of Composition**:

8. One paragraph per topic
9. Begin paragraph with topic sentence
10. **Use active voice**
11. **Put statements in positive form**
12. **Use definite, specific, concrete language**
13. **Omit needless words**
14. Avoid succession of loose sentences
15. Express co-ordinate ideas in similar form
16. **Keep related words together**
17. Keep to one tense in summaries
18. **Place emphatic words at end of sentence**

### Reference Files

The rules above are summarized from Strunk's original text. For complete explanations with examples:

| Section | File | ~Tokens |
|---------|------|---------|
| Grammar, punctuation, comma rules | `02-elementary-rules-of-usage.md` | 2,500 |
| Paragraph structure, active voice, concision | `03-elementary-principles-of-composition.md` | 4,500 |
| Headings, quotations, formatting | `04-a-few-matters-of-form.md` | 1,000 |
| Word choice, common errors | `05-words-and-expressions-commonly-misused.md` | 4,000 |

**Most tasks need only `03-elementary-principles-of-composition.md`** — it covers active voice, positive form, concrete language, and omitting needless words.

## AI Writing Patterns to Avoid

LLMs regress to statistical means, producing generic, puffy prose. Avoid:

- **Puffery:** pivotal, crucial, vital, testament, enduring legacy
- **Empty "-ing" phrases:** ensuring reliability, showcasing features, highlighting capabilities
- **Promotional adjectives:** groundbreaking, seamless, robust, cutting-edge
- **Overused AI vocabulary:** delve, leverage, multifaceted, foster, realm, tapestry
- **Em dashes:** never use em dashes (— or --); restructure the sentence instead
- **Formatting overuse:** excessive bullets, emoji decorations, bold on every other word

Be specific, not grandiose. Say what it actually does.

For comprehensive research on why these patterns occur, see `signs-of-ai-writing.md`. Wikipedia editors developed this guide to detect AI-generated submissions — their patterns are well-documented and field-tested.

## Bottom Line

Writing for humans? Load the relevant section from `elements-of-style/` and apply the rules. For most tasks, `03-elementary-principles-of-composition.md` covers what matters most.

Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\vale-user-facing-text

# Vale Validation For User-Facing Text

This skill ships its own Vale configuration as a bundled artifact so it works
consistently regardless of whether the project has its own `.vale.ini`.

## Bundled Config

The embedded config lives at `config/.vale.ini` inside this skill's directory.
It declares the style packages and targets all Markdown files. Vale downloads
the style packages into `config/.styles/` next to it, keeping everything
self-contained.

Always pass `--config <SKILL_DIR>/config/.vale.ini` to every Vale invocation.
Never rely on the project's own `.vale.ini` or omit the flag.

## Resolving `<SKILL_DIR>`

`<SKILL_DIR>` is the directory that contains this `SKILL.md` file. Determine
it at runtime from the path the skill was loaded from. Examples:

- Local skill: `skills/vale-user-facing-text`
- APM module: `apm_modules/<vendor>/agentic/skills/vale-user-facing-text`

## When To Apply

Apply this skill whenever creating or editing user-facing text, including:

- Markdown documentation (`.md`, `.mdx`)
- Code comments and docstrings that explain behavior to humans
- Changelogs, release notes, migration notes, and onboarding guides
- Any generated informational text intended for users

## First-Run Setup

Before running Vale, check whether `config/.styles/` exists under `<SKILL_DIR>`.
If that folder is missing, run sync first. Do not run validation commands until
sync has completed successfully.

When `config/.styles/` does not exist:

```shell
vale sync --config <SKILL_DIR>/config/.vale.ini
```

After sync, the downloaded styles are stored in `<SKILL_DIR>/config/.styles/`.
For later runs, skip sync if that folder already exists.

## Validation Workflow

1. Identify all touched files containing user-facing prose. This includes:
   - `.md` and `.mdx` files edited or created in this change
   - Source files (`.go`, `.py`, `.ts`, etc.) that contain comments or docstrings
     with human-readable explanations rather than code identifiers
2. Ensure style packages are present: if `<SKILL_DIR>/config/.styles/` is absent,
   run `vale sync --config <SKILL_DIR>/config/.vale.ini` before proceeding.
3. Run Vale against each edited Markdown file:

```shell
vale --config <SKILL_DIR>/config/.vale.ini <edited-file>
```

4. For user-facing prose in non-Markdown locations (such as inline code
  comments), copy the prose into a temporary `.md` file and validate it.
  Name the temp file `_vale_tmp.md` and place it next to the source file.
  Delete it once validation is complete, never commit it.

```shell
vale --config <SKILL_DIR>/config/.vale.ini _vale_tmp.md
```

5. Apply the wording fixes back to the source while preserving technical
  accuracy and intent.
6. Re-run Vale until remaining findings are resolved or intentionally kept with
  a documented reason.

## Handling Findings

- Fix clarity, tone, and wording issues first.
- Prefer concise, direct phrasing over verbose language.
- Keep domain-specific terms and code identifiers unchanged when required.
- If a suggestion conflicts with correctness, keep the correct wording and
  briefly note the reason in your response.

## Completion Checklist

- [ ] Style packages are present in `config/.styles/`
- [ ] Every edited user-facing text section was validated with the bundled config
- [ ] Vale findings were resolved or explicitly justified
- [ ] Final prose remains technically accurate and audience-appropriate

# Asynchronous prompt updates: what Oh My Posh needs from Nushell Oh My Posh has a feature called [streaming](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/configuration/streaming): the prompt renders immediately, slow segments (git status in a large repo, a network call, a language version lookup) show a placeholder, and the prompt updates in place as each segment resolves. It works today in PowerShell, zsh, fish, and cmd (Clink). We would like to support it in Nushell, but the integration requires two capabilities the Nushell REPL currently does not expose. This document explains how the feature works, how the current Nushell integration is structured, and what we would need from Nushell (and reedline) to make it work. ## What streaming does Users enable it with a single config property, the per-segment time budget in milliseconds: ```json { "streaming": 100 } ``` With this set, Oh My Posh renders the prompt as follows: 1. Every segment starts executing concurrently, each with a 100ms budget. 2. Segments that finish in time render normally. Segments that exceed the budget render their `placeholder` (default `...`) and keep executing in the background. 3. The full prompt, placeholders included, is emitted immediately. The user can start typing right away. 4. Each time a pending segment completes, the full prompt is re-rendered and emitted again. 5. The shell repaints the prompt in place with each update, without disturbing what the user has typed. ## The division of labor Oh My Posh does all the heavy lifting. The shell integration is deliberately thin. **Oh My Posh handles:** segment execution and timeouts, rendering complete prompt strings (never diffs or partial updates), caching, and process lifetime (the stream process exits on its own once every segment has resolved). **The shell must provide exactly three things:** 1. **Spawn and read:** start `oh-my-posh stream ...` in the background and read its stdout without blocking the REPL. 2. **Repaint on data:** when a new record arrives while the user sits at the prompt, replace the displayed prompt with the new string and repaint, preserving the edit buffer and cursor position. 3. **Stop on accept:** when the user presses Enter (or Ctrl-C), stop consuming updates so a late record does not paint over command output. Requirement 2 is the crux, and it is the one Nushell currently has no mechanism for. ### The wire protocol `oh-my-posh stream --shell=nu --status=... --execution-time=... ...` writes null-byte (`\0`) delimited records to stdout. Each record is one of: - a complete, fully escaped primary prompt string, ready to display as-is, or - a transient prompt (prefixed with a `\x1e` marker byte), which the shell caches so that rendering the transient prompt on Enter needs no extra process spawn. The first record arrives immediately (it is the prompt with placeholders) and is consumed synchronously, so the prompt never appears late. Subsequent records arrive as segments resolve. The process exits when everything is resolved, so a fast config produces a single record and exits at once. There is also a persistent-daemon variant (`oh-my-posh serve`) that removes the process spawn per prompt, but it is an optimization on top of the same record protocol. The per-prompt `stream` process is the minimal integration and what this request is about. ## How the four supported shells do it Each shell uses whatever async primitive and repaint API it has: | Shell | Async notification | Repaint call | | ---------- | --------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | | zsh | `zle -F handler` (fd watcher inside the line editor) | set `PS1`, then `zle reset-prompt` | | fish | background reader process signals the shell with `SIGUSR1` | set prompt variable, then `commandline -f repaint` | | PowerShell | `PowerShell.OnIdle` engine event drains a shared queue | `[Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()` | | cmd | Clink Lua coroutines poll the daemon | `clink.refilterprompt()` | In every case the repaint API redraws the prompt while keeping the user's partially typed command line intact. That API is the load-bearing piece. ### Sequence flow (zsh as the reference implementation) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant S as Shell (line editor) participant O as oh-my-posh stream S->>O: spawn with context (status, duration, pwd, ...) O-->>S: record 1: prompt with "..." placeholders Note over S: blocking read, ~instant S->>U: display prompt U->>S: starts typing Note over O: git segment resolves after 400ms O-->>S: record 2: full prompt, git segment filled in Note over S: fd watcher fires (zle -F) S->>S: PS1 = record 2, zle reset-prompt Note over U: prompt updates in place,
typed input untouched O-->>S: record 3 (\x1e prefix): transient prompt S->>S: cache transient prompt Note over O: all segments resolved O-->>S: EOF, process exits U->>S: presses Enter S->>S: close fd (stops any in-flight stream),
print cached transient prompt S->>U: command executes ``` ## Where this touches the current Nushell integration Today's init script (generated by `oh-my-posh init nu`) is fully synchronous. The relevant part: ```nu $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| # gather context (cleared screen detection, SET_POSHCONTEXT) ... _omp_get_prompt primary $"--cleared=($clear)" } $env.PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT = {|| _omp_get_prompt right } ``` `_omp_get_prompt` calls `^oh-my-posh print primary ...` and blocks until every segment has finished. A slow git repository holds the entire prompt hostage. This closure is exactly where the streaming path would slot in: spawn `oh-my-posh stream` instead, return the first record, and let later records repaint. The rest of the feature already maps cleanly onto existing Nushell hooks: - **Transient prompt** works today via `$env.TRANSIENT_PROMPT_COMMAND`; the streamed transient record would only make it cheaper (serve it from cache instead of spawning a process on every Enter). - **Stop on accept** (requirement 3) maps onto the existing `pre_execution` hook: kill the stream process there. ## What Nushell is missing Nushell evaluates `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` once per prompt cycle, converts the result to a string, and hands it to reedline as static prompt content before `read_line()` starts. From that point until the line is submitted, two things are impossible: 1. **Nothing external can wake the REPL.** `job spawn` gives us a background thread that could read the stream, and `job send 0` can queue a message for the main thread, but the main thread is parked inside `read_line()` and only processes key events. There is no fd watcher, no signal handler, no timer, and no job-message delivery that fires while the editor waits for input. (`SIGUSR1`, which the fish integration relies on, would not fit Nushell's cross-platform model anyway; a channel-based wake fits better.) 2. **Prompt content cannot change mid-edit.** Even if a repaint happens (reedline has `ReedlineEvent::Repaint`, though it is only reachable through a keybinding), reedline re-queries the `Prompt` trait, and Nushell's implementation returns the same stored string. There is no way to say "the prompt is now this new string, redraw it" while the buffer stays intact. Notably, reedline already contains most of the required plumbing in another feature: the `ExternalPrinter` is a channel that background threads write to, which reedline polls during `read_line()` and which triggers a safe redraw (print above the prompt, restore the buffer). Asynchronous prompt updates need the same wake-and-redraw cycle with one difference: the payload replaces the prompt instead of printing above it. ## The ask A mechanism for a background job to replace the prompt while `read_line()` is active, preserving the edit buffer and cursor. Two shapes would work for us, in order of preference: ### Option A: push-based prompt updates (preferred) Expose a way for a background job to hand the REPL a pre-rendered prompt string and trigger a repaint, for example a `commandline` subcommand: ```nu commandline set-prompt --left $new_prompt # swap content + repaint, buffer untouched ``` Internally this could mirror the `ExternalPrinter` design: a bounded channel created by the host, polled in reedline's `read_line()` event loop; on receipt, update the prompt content held by the host's `Prompt` implementation and repaint. Oh My Posh sends complete prompt strings, so nothing needs re-evaluation, and this stays independent of `PROMPT_COMMAND` semantics. ### Option B: externally triggered prompt re-evaluation A way for a background job to request a full prompt refresh, where Nushell re-runs `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` (and `PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT`) and repaints in place. This also works, but costs a closure evaluation per update and has trickier semantics (the closure runs on the engine while a command line is being edited). ### What the resulting integration would look like With Option A, the streaming path in `omp.nu` would be roughly: ```nu $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| # spawn the stream; read records in the background job spawn { ^$_omp_executable stream --shell=nu ...context... | split row (char --integer 0) # \0 delimited records | each {|record| if ($record | str starts-with (char --integer 30)) { # \x1e prefix: cache the transient prompt for later $env._omp_transient = ($record | str substring 1..) } else { commandline set-prompt --left $record # <- the missing capability } } } # ... return the first record synchronously so the prompt shows instantly } ``` (Exact plumbing aside: the point is that everything except `commandline set-prompt` is expressible in Nushell today.) ### Sequence flow of the proposed integration ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant R as reedline (read_line) participant N as Nushell engine participant J as background job participant O as oh-my-posh stream N->>N: PROMPT_COMMAND runs N->>O: spawn stream O-->>N: record 1 (placeholders) N->>J: job spawn (reader) N->>R: read_line(prompt = record 1) R->>U: display prompt U->>R: starts typing O-->>J: record 2 (segment resolved) J->>R: prompt-update channel: record 2 Note over R: poll wakes, swap prompt, repaint,
buffer and cursor preserved R->>U: updated prompt, input intact U->>R: presses Enter R->>N: Signal::Success N->>J: pre_execution hook: job kill (stops stream) ``` ## Guarantees from our side To keep the shell-side risk low, the Oh My Posh contract is strict: - Every record is a complete prompt string, already escaped for the target shell. The shell never assembles or diffs anything. - Records are strictly newer-wins; applying only the latest received record is always correct, so a slow consumer can coalesce updates. - The stream process exits on its own once all segments resolve, and dies on SIGPIPE/EOF if the shell goes away, so there is nothing to reap in the happy path. - If spawning or reading fails, the integration falls back to today's synchronous rendering. Users without `streaming` in their config keep the exact current behavior. We are happy to build and maintain the Nushell side of the integration (as we do for the other shells) and to adjust our protocol if a different API shape lands. The single capability that unblocks this, and likely every other async-prompt tool in the ecosystem (starship and friends have the same limitation), is: **let a background job replace the prompt and repaint while the line editor is active.**

With this set, Oh My Posh renders the prompt as follows:

1. Every segment starts executing concurrently, each with a 100ms budget.
2. Segments that finish in time render normally. Segments that exceed the budget render their `placeholder`
   (default `...`) and keep executing in the background.
3. The full prompt, placeholders included, is emitted immediately. The user can start typing right away.
4. Each time a pending segment completes, the full prompt is re-rendered and emitted again.
5. The shell repaints the prompt in place with each update, without disturbing what the user has typed.

The same setting also enables a long-lived `oh-my-posh serve` daemon per shell session. The daemon renders every
prompt on request, which removes the process spawn per prompt (the largest prompt cost on Windows) and keeps segment
caches warm in memory. Both mechanisms emit the same record format; they differ only in transport and lifetime.

input.new_string:
**The shell must provide four things:**

1. **Spawn and read:** start the renderer (the daemon at init, or a `stream` process per prompt) in the background and
   read its stdout without blocking the REPL.
2. **Request per prompt (daemon mode):** send the daemon a render request with fresh context when a new prompt is due.
3. **Repaint on data:** when a new record arrives while the user sits at the prompt, replace the displayed prompt with
   the new string and repaint, preserving the edit buffer and cursor position.
4. **Stop on accept:** when the user presses Enter (or Ctrl-C), stop consuming updates so a late record does not paint
   over command output (kill the `stream` process, or send the daemon an `abort`).

Requirement 3 is the crux, and it is the one Nushell currently has no mechanism for.

input.old_string:
### The wire protocol

`oh-my-posh stream --shell=nu --status=... --execution-time=... ...` writes null-byte (`\0`) delimited records to
stdout. Each record is one of:

- a complete, fully escaped primary prompt string, ready to display as-is, or
- a transient prompt (prefixed with a `\x1e` marker byte), which the shell caches so that rendering the transient
  prompt on Enter needs no extra process spawn.

The first record arrives immediately (it is the prompt with placeholders) and is consumed synchronously, so the prompt
shows without delay. Subsequent records arrive as segments resolve. The process exits when everything is resolved, so a
fast config produces a single record and exits at once.

There is also a persistent-daemon variant (`oh-my-posh serve`) that removes the process spawn per prompt, but it is an
optimization on top of the same record protocol. The per-prompt `stream` process is the minimal integration and what
this request is about.
input.new_string:
### The wire protocol

Both mechanisms write null-byte (`\0`) delimited records to stdout. Each record is one of:

- a complete, fully escaped primary prompt string, ready to display as-is, or
- a transient prompt (prefixed with a `\x1e` marker byte), which the shell caches so that rendering the transient
  prompt on Enter needs no extra process spawn.

The first record arrives immediately (it is the prompt with placeholders) and is consumed synchronously, so the prompt
shows without delay. Subsequent records arrive as segments resolve.

**Per-prompt stream.** `oh-my-posh stream --shell=nu --status=... --execution-time=... ...` receives its context as
CLI flags, streams records for one prompt cycle, and exits once every segment has resolved. A fast config produces a
single record and exits at once.

**Session daemon.** `oh-my-posh serve --shell=nu` starts once at shell init and lives for the session. Each prompt,
the shell sends one JSON line, either to the daemon's stdin (zsh feeds it through a coprocess, PowerShell through the
process object's stdin) or to a named pipe (`--request-pipe`, used by fish):

```json
{"command":"render","id":42,"status":0,"execution-time":312,"pwd":"...","terminal-width":120,"env":{"...":"..."}}
```

Records come back on stdout in the same format as `stream`, except each payload is prefixed with the request id and a
`\x1f` separator (`42\x1f<prompt>\0`) so the shell can discard records from a superseded cycle. Two more commands
complete the protocol: `abort` cancels the in-flight render cycle (sent on Enter), and `quit` shuts the daemon down
(sent on shell exit; losing its stdout pipe also terminates it, so a crashed shell never leaks a daemon).

When streaming is enabled, the generated init scripts prefer the daemon, fall back to a per-prompt `stream` process
when it fails (the daemon is abandoned for the session after three failures), and fall back to classic synchronous
rendering when both are unavailable.

  without `streaming` in their config keep the exact current behavior.

What changed:

- **The wire protocol section** describes both mechanisms: the per-prompt `stream` process (context via CLI flags, exits when resolved) and the `serve` daemon — one JSON line per prompt (`{"command":"render","id":42,...}`) over stdin or a named pipe (`--request-pipe`), id-prefixed records (`42\x1f<prompt>\0`) so stale cycles are discardable, plus the `abort` and `quit` commands and the stdout-pipe-closes-kills-it lifecycle. It also spells out the fallback chain: daemon → per-prompt stream → synchronous.

- **A second sequence diagram** shows the daemon cycle: render request, placeholder record, in-place updates with id matching, cached transient, `abort` on Enter, daemon surviving into the next prompt.
- **The Nushell mapping** now says the daemon starts once at init (next to where omp.nu renders `PROMPT_MULTILINE_INDICATOR`), `PROMPT_COMMAND` sends the render request with `stream` as fallback, `pre_execution` sends `abort`, and no exit hook is needed because the daemon dies with its stdout pipe. The proposed-integration sketch notes the daemon fits the same shape — a session-long `job spawn` instead of a per-prompt one, reusing the named-pipe transport fish already needed for the same reason (fish can't write to a running process's stdin either).

The core ask to Nushell is unchanged, which is the right message: both mechanisms converge on the same single missing capability — letting a background job replace the prompt and repaint while `read_line()` is active.

1	## III. Elementary Principles Of Composition
2	
3	### Rule 8. Make the paragraph the unit of composition: one paragraph to each topic.
4	
5	If the subject on which you are writing is of slight extent, or if you intend to treat it very briefly, there may be no need of subdividing it into topics. Thus a brief description, a brief summary of a literary work, a brief account of a single incident, a narrative merely outlining an action, the setting forth of a single idea, any one of these is best written in a single paragraph. After the paragraph has been written, examine it to see whether subdivision will not improve it.
6	
7	Ordinarily, however, a subject requires subdivision into topics, each of which should be made the subject of a paragraph. The object of treating each topic in a paragraph by itself is, of course, to aid the reader. The beginning of each paragraph is a signal to him that a new step in the development of the subject has been reached.
8	
9	The extent of subdivision will vary with the length of the composition. For example, a short notice of a book or poem might consist of a single paragraph. One slightly longer might consist of two paragraphs:
10	
11	- A. Account of the work.
12	- B. Critical discussion.
13	
14	A report on a poem, written for a class in literature, might consist of seven paragraphs:
15	
16	- A. Facts of composition and publication.
17	- B. Kind of poem; metrical form.
18	- C. Subject.
19	- D. Treatment of subject.
20	- E. For what chiefly remarkable.
21	- F. Wherein characteristic of the writer.
22	- G. Relationship to other works.
23	
24	The contents of paragraphs C and D would vary with the poem. Usually, paragraph C would indicate the actual or imagined circumstances of the poem (the situation), if these call for explanation, and would then state the subject and outline its development. If the poem is a narrative in the third person throughout, paragraph C need contain no more than a concise summary of the action. Paragraph D would indicate the leading ideas and show how they are made prominent, or would indicate what points in the narrative are chiefly emphasized.
25	
26	A novel might be discussed under the heads:
27	
28	- A. Setting.
29	- B. Plot.
30	- C. Characters.
31	- D. Purpose.
32	
33	An historical event might be discussed under the heads:
34	
35	- A. What led up to the event.
36	- B. Account of the event.
37	- C. What the event led up to.
38	
39	In treating either of these last two subjects, the writer would probably find it necessary to subdivide one or more of the topics here given.
40	
41	As a rule, single sentences should not be written or printed as paragraphs. An exception may be made of sentences of transition, indicating the relation between the parts of an exposition or argument. Frequent exceptions are also necessary in textbooks, guidebooks, and other works in which many topics are treated briefly.
42	
43	In dialogue, each speech, even if only a single word, is a paragraph by itself; that is, a new paragraph begins with each change of speaker. The application of this rule, when dialogue and narrative are combined, is best learned from examples in well-printed works of fiction.
44	
45	### Rule 9. As a rule, begin each paragraph with a topic sentence, end it in conformity with the beginning.
46	
47	Again, the object is to aid the reader. The practice here recommended enables him to discover the purpose of each paragraph as he begins to read it, and to retain this purpose in mind as he ends it. For this reason, the most generally useful kind of paragraph, particularly in exposition and argument, is that in which
48	
49	\(a\) the topic sentence comes at or near the beginning;
50	
51	\(b\) the succeeding sentences explain or establish or develop the statement made in the topic sentence; and
52	
53	\(c\) the final sentence either emphasizes the thought of the topic sentence or states some important consequence.
54	
55	Ending with a digression, or with an unimportant detail, is particularly to be avoided.
56	
57	If the paragraph forms part of a larger composition, its relation to what precedes, or its function as a part of the whole, may need to be expressed. This can sometimes be done by a mere word or phrase (*again*; *therefore*; *for the same reason*) in the topic sentence. Sometimes, however, it is expedient to precede the topic sentence by one or more sentences of introduction or transition. If more than one such sentence is required, it is generally better to set apart the transitional sentences as a separate paragraph.
58	
59	According to the writer's purpose, he may, as indicated above, relate the body of the paragraph to the topic sentence in one or more of several different ways. He may make the meaning of the topic sentence clearer by restating it in other forms, by defining its terms, by denying the contrary, by giving illustrations or specific instances; he may establish it by proofs; or he may develop it by showing its implications and consequences. In a long paragraph, he may carry out several of these processes.
60	
61	1 Now, to be properly enjoyed, a walking tour should be gone upon alone. 2 If you go in a company, or even in pairs, it is no longer a walking tour in anything but name; it is something else and more in the nature of a picnic. 3 A walking tour should be gone upon alone, because freedom is of the essence; because you should be able to stop and go on, and follow this way or that, as the freak takes you; and because you must have your own pace, and neither trot alongside a champion walker, nor mince in time with a girl. 4 And you must be open to all impressions and let your thoughts take colour from what you see. 5 You should be as a pipe for any wind to play upon. 6 “I cannot see the wit,” says Hazlitt, “of walking and talking at the same time. 7 When I am in the country, I wish to vegetate like the country,” which is the gist of all that can be said upon the matter. 8 There should be no cackle of voices at your elbow, to jar on the meditative silence of the morning. 9 And so long as a man is reasoning he cannot surrender himself to that fine intoxication that comes of much motion in the open air, that begins in a sort of dazzle and sluggishness of the brain, and ends in a peace that passes comprehension.—Stevenson, Walking Tours.
62	
63	1 Topic sentence. 2 The meaning made clearer by denial of the contrary. 3 The topic sentence repeated, in abridged form, and supported by three reasons; the meaning of the third (“you must have your own pace”) made clearer by denying the contrary. 4 A fourth reason, stated in two forms. 5 The same reason, stated in still another form. 6–7 The same reason as stated by Hazlitt. 8 Repetition, in paraphrase, of the quotation from Hazlitt. 9 Final statement of the fourth reason, in language amplified and heightened to form a strong conclusion.
64	
65	1 It was chiefly in the eighteenth century that a very different conception of history grew up. 2 Historians then came to believe that their task was not so much to paint a picture as to solve a problem; to explain or illustrate the successive phases of national growth, prosperity, and adversity. 3 The history of morals, of industry, of intellect, and of art; the changes that take place in manners or beliefs; the dominant ideas that prevailed in successive periods; the rise, fall, and modification of political constitutions; in a word, all the conditions of national well-being became the subject of their works. 4 They sought rather to write a history of peoples than a history of kings. 5 They looked especially in history for the chain of causes and effects. 6 They undertook to study in the past the physiology of nations, and hoped by applying the experimental method on a large scale to deduce some lessons of real value about the conditions on which the welfare of society mainly depend.—Lecky, The Political Value of History.
66	
67	1 Topic sentence. 2 The meaning of the topic sentence made clearer; the new conception of history defined. 3 The definition expanded. 4 The definition explained by contrast. 5 The definition supplemented: another element in the new conception of history. 6 Conclusion: an important consequence of the new conception of history.
68	
69	In narration and description the paragraph sometimes begins with a concise, comprehensive statement serving to hold together the details that follow.
70	
71	The breeze served us admirably.
72	
73	The campaign opened with a series of reverses.
74	
75	The next ten or twelve pages were filled with a curious set of entries.
76	
77	But this device, if too often used, would become a mannerism. More commonly the opening sentence simply indicates by its subject with what the paragraph is to be principally concerned.
78	
79	At length I thought I might return towards the stockade.
80	
81	He picked up the heavy lamp from the table and began to explore.
82	
83	Another flight of steps, and they emerged on the roof.
84	
85	The brief paragraphs of animated narrative, however, are often without even this semblance of a topic sentence. The break between them serves the purpose of a rhetorical pause, throwing into prominence some detail of the action.
86	
87	### Rule 10. Use the active voice.
88	
89	The active voice is usually more direct and vigorous than the passive:
90	
91	I shall always remember my first visit to Boston.
92	
93	This is much better than
94	
95	My first visit to Boston will always be remembered by me.
96	
97	The latter sentence is less direct, less bold, and less concise. If the writer tries to make it more concise by omitting “by me,”
98	
99	My first visit to Boston will always be remembered,
100	
101	it becomes indefinite: is it the writer, or some person undisclosed, or the world at large, that will always remember this visit?
102	
103	This rule does not, of course, mean that the writer should entirely discard the passive voice, which is frequently convenient and sometimes necessary.
104	
105	The dramatists of the Restoration are little esteemed to-day.
106	
107	Modern readers have little esteem for the dramatists of the Restoration.
108	
109	The first would be the right form in a paragraph on the dramatists of the Restoration; the second, in a paragraph on the tastes of modern readers. The need of making a particular word the subject of the sentence will often, as in these examples, determine which voice is to be used.
110	
111	As a rule, avoid making one passive depend directly upon another.
112	
113	| Original | Revision |
114	| --- | --- |
115	| Gold was not allowed to be exported. | It was forbidden to export gold (The export of gold was prohibited). |
116	| He has been proved to have been seen entering the building. | It has been proved that he was seen to enter the building. |
117	
118	In both the examples above, before correction, the word properly related to the second passive is made the subject of the first.
119	
120	A common fault is to use as the subject of a passive construction a noun which expresses the entire action, leaving to the verb no function beyond that of completing the sentence.
121	
122	| Original | Revision |
123	| --- | --- |
124	| A survey of this region was made in 1900. | This region was surveyed in 1900. |
125	| Mobilization of the army was rapidly effected. | The army was rapidly mobilized. |
126	| Confirmation of these reports cannot be obtained. | These reports cannot be confirmed. |
127	
128	Compare the _sentence,_ “The export of gold was prohibited,” in which the predicate “was prohibited” expresses something not implied in “export.”
129	
130	The habitual use of the active voice makes for forcible writing. This is true not only in narrative principally concerned with action, but in writing of any kind. Many a tame sentence of description or exposition can be made lively and emphatic by substituting a verb in the active voice for some such perfunctory expression as *there is*, or *could be heard*.
131	
132	| Original | Revision |
133	| --- | --- |
134	| There were a great number of dead leaves lying on the ground. | Dead leaves covered the ground. |
135	| The sound of a guitar somewhere in the house could be heard. | Somewhere in the house a guitar hummed sleepily. |
136	| The reason that he left college was that his health became impaired. | Failing health compelled him to leave college. |
137	| It was not long before he was very sorry that he had said what he had. | He soon repented his words. |
138	
139	### Rule 11. Put statements in positive form.
140	
141	Make definite assertions. Avoid tame, colorless, hesitating, non-committal language. Use the word *not* as a means of denial or in antithesis, never as a means of evasion.
142	
143	| Original | Revision |
144	| --- | --- |
145	| He was not very often on time. | He usually came late. |
146	| He did not think that studying Latin was much use. | He thought the study of Latin useless. |
147	| The Taming of the Shrew is rather weak in spots. Shakespeare does not portray Katharine as a very admirable character, nor does Bianca remain long in memory as an important character in Shakespeare's works. | The women in The Taming of the Shrew are unattractive. Katharine is disagreeable, Bianca insignificant. |
148	
149	The last example, before correction, is indefinite as well as negative. The corrected version, consequently, is simply a guess at the writer's intention.
150	
151	All three examples show the weakness inherent in the word *not*. Consciously or unconsciously, the reader is dissatisfied with being told only what is not; he wishes to be told what is. Hence, as a rule, it is better to express even a negative in positive form.
152	
153	| Original | Revision |
154	| --- | --- |
155	| not honest | dishonest |
156	| not important | trifling |
157	| did not remember | forgot |
158	| did not pay any attention to | ignored |
159	| did not have much confidence in | distrusted |
160	
161	The antithesis of negative and positive is strong:
162	
163	Not charity, but simple justice.
164	
165	Not that I loved Caesar less, but Rome the more.
166	
167	Negative words other than *not* are usually strong:
168	
169	The sun never sets upon the British flag.
170	
171	### Rule 12. Use definite, specific, concrete language.
172	
173	Prefer the specific to the general, the definite to the vague, the concrete to the abstract.
174	
175	| Original | Revision |
176	| --- | --- |
177	| A period of unfavorable weather set in. | It rained every day for a week. |
178	| He showed satisfaction as he took possession of his well-earned reward. | He grinned as he pocketed the coin. |
179	| There is a general agreement among those who have enjoyed the experience that surf-riding is productive of great exhilaration. | All who have tried surf-riding agree that it is most exhilarating. |
180	
181	If those who have studied the art of writing are in accord on any one point, it is on this, that the surest method of arousing and holding the attention of the reader is by being specific, definite, and concrete. Critics have pointed out how much of the effectiveness of the greatest writers, Homer, Dante, Shakespeare, results from their constant definiteness and concreteness. Browning, to cite a more modern author, affords many striking examples. Take, for instance, the lines from My Last Duchess,
182	
183	Sir, 'twas all one! My favour at her breast,
184	
185	The dropping of the daylight in the west,
186	
187	The bough of cherries some officious fool
188	
189	Broke in the orchard for her, the white mule
190	
191	She rode with round the terrace—all and each
192	
193	Would draw from her alike the approving speech,
194	
195	Or blush, at least,
196	
197	and those which end the poem,
198	
199	Notice Neptune, though,
200	
201	Taming a sea-horse, thought a rarity,
202	
203	Which Claus of Innsbruck cast in bronze for me.
204	
205	These words call up pictures. Recall how in The Bishop Orders his Tomb in St. Praxed's Church “the Renaissance spirit—its worldliness, inconsistency, pride, hypocrisy, ignorance of itself, love of art, of luxury, of good Latin,” to quote Ruskin's comment on the poem, is made manifest in specific details and in concrete terms.
206	
207	Prose, in particular narrative and descriptive prose, is made vivid by the same means. If the experiences of Jim Hawkins and of David Balfour, of Kim, of Nostromo, have seemed for the moment real to countless readers, if in reading Carlyle we have almost the sense of being physically present at the taking of the Bastille, it is because of the definiteness of the details and the concreteness of the terms used. It is not that every detail is given; that would be impossible, as well as to no purpose; but that all the significant details are given, and not vaguely, but with such definiteness that the reader, in imagination, can project himself into the scene.
208	
209	In exposition and in argument, the writer must likewise never lose his hold upon the concrete, and even when he is dealing with general principles, he must give particular instances of their application.
210	
211	“This superiority of specific expressions is clearly due to the effort required to translate words into thoughts. As we do not think in generals, but in particulars—as whenever any class of things is referred to, we represent it to ourselves by calling to mind individual members of it, it follows that when an abstract word is used, the hearer or reader has to choose, from his stock of images, one or more by which he may figure to himself the genus mentioned. In doing this, some delay must arise, some force be expended; and if by employing a specific term an appropriate image can be at once suggested, an economy is achieved, and a more vivid impression produced.”
212	
213	Herbert Spencer, from whose Philosophy of Style the preceding paragraph is quoted, illustrates the principle by the sentences:
214	
215	| Original | Revision |
216	| --- | --- |
217	| In proportion as the manners, customs, and amusements of a nation are cruel and barbarous, the regulations of their penal code will be severe. | In proportion as men delight in battles, bull-fights, and combats of gladiators, will they punish by hanging, burning, and the rack. |
218	
219	### Rule 13. Omit needless words.
220	
221	Vigorous writing is concise. A sentence should contain no unnecessary words, a paragraph no unnecessary sentences, for the same reason that a drawing should have no unnecessary lines and a machine no unnecessary parts. This requires not that the writer make all his sentences short, or that he avoid all detail and treat his subjects only in outline, but that he make every word tell.
222	
223	Many expressions in common use violate this principle:
224	
225	| Original | Revision |
226	| --- | --- |
227	| the question as to whether | whether (the question whether) |
228	| there is no doubt but that | no doubt (doubtless) |
229	| used for fuel purposes | used for fuel |
230	| he is a man who | he |
231	| in a hasty manner | hastily |
232	| this is a subject which | this subject |
233	| His story is a strange one. | His story is strange. |
234	
235	In especial the expression *the fact that* should be revised out of every sentence in which it occurs.
236	
237	| Original | Revision |
238	| --- | --- |
239	| owing to the fact that | since (because) |
240	| in spite of the fact that | though (although) |
241	| call your attention to the fact that | remind you (notify you) |
242	| I was unaware of the fact that | I was unaware that (did not know) |
243	| the fact that he had not succeeded | his failure |
244	| the fact that I had arrived | my arrival |
245	
246	See also under *case*, *character*, *nature*, *system* in Chapter V.
247	
248	*Who is*, *which was*, and the like are often superfluous.
249	
250	| Original | Revision |
251	| --- | --- |
252	| His brother, who is a member of the same firm | His brother, a member of the same firm |
253	| Trafalgar, which was Nelson's last battle | Trafalgar, Nelson's last battle |
254	
255	As positive statement is more concise than negative, and the active voice more concise than the passive, many of the examples given under Rules 11 and 12 illustrate this rule as well.
256	
257	A common violation of conciseness is the presentation of a single complex idea, step by step, in a series of sentences or independent clauses which might to advantage be combined into one.
258	
259	| Original | Revision |
260	| --- | --- |
261	| Macbeth was very ambitious. This led him to wish to become king of Scotland. The witches told him that this wish of his would come true. The king of Scotland at this time was Duncan. Encouraged by his wife, Macbeth murdered Duncan. He was thus enabled to succeed Duncan as king. (51 words.) | Encouraged by his wife, Macbeth achieved his ambition and realized the prediction of the witches by murdering Duncan and becoming king of Scotland in his place. (26 words.) |
262	| There were several less important courses, but these were the most important, and although they did not come every day, they came often enough to keep you in such a state of mind that you never knew what your next move would be. (43 words.) | These, the most important courses of all, came, if not daily, at least often enough to keep one under constant strain. (21 words.) |
263	
264	### Rule 14. Avoid a succession of loose sentences
265	
266	This rule refers especially to loose sentences of a particular type, those consisting of two co-ordinate clauses, the second introduced by a conjunction or relative. Although single sentences of this type may be unexceptionable (see under Rule 4), a series soon becomes monotonous and tedious.
267	
268	An unskilful writer will sometimes construct a whole paragraph of sentences of this kind, using as connectives *and*, *but*, *so*, and less frequently, *who*, *which*, *when*, *where*, and *while*, these last in non-restrictive senses (see under Rule 3).
269	
270	The third concert of the subscription series was given last evening, and a large audience was in attendance. Mr. Edward Appleton was the soloist, and the Boston Symphony Orchestra furnished the instrumental music. The former showed himself to be an artist of the first rank, while the latter proved itself fully deserving of its high reputation. The interest aroused by the series has been very gratifying to the Committee, and it is planned to give a similar series annually hereafter. The fourth concert will be given on Tuesday, May 10, when an equally attractive programme will be presented.
271	
272	Apart from its triteness and emptiness, the paragraph above is weak because of the structure of its sentences, with their mechanical symmetry and sing-song. Contrast with them the sentences in the paragraphs quoted under Rule 9, or in any piece of good English prose, as the preface (Before the Curtain) to Vanity Fair.
273	
274	If the writer finds that he has written a series of sentences of the type described, he should recast enough of them to remove the monotony, replacing them by simple sentences, by sentences of two clauses joined by a semicolon, by periodic sentences of two clauses, by sentences, loose or periodic, of three clauses—whichever best represent the real relations of the thought.
275	
276	### Rule 15. Express co-ordinate ideas in similar form.
277	
278	This principle, that of parallel construction, requires that expressions of similar content and function should be outwardly similar. The likeness of form enables the reader to recognize more readily the likeness of content and function. Familiar instances from the Bible are the Ten Commandments, the Beatitudes, and the petitions of the Lord's Prayer.
279	
280	The unskillful writer often violates this principle, from a mistaken belief that he should constantly vary the form of his expressions. It is true that in repeating a statement in order to emphasize it he may have need to vary its form. For illustration, see the paragraph from Stevenson quoted under Rule _9_. But apart from this, he should follow the principle of parallel construction.
281	
282	| Original | Revision |
283	| --- | --- |
284	| Formerly, science was taught by the textbook method, while now the laboratory method is employed. | Formerly, science was taught by the textbook method; now it is taught by the laboratory method. |
285	
286	The left-hand version gives the impression that the writer is undecided or timid; he seems unable or afraid to choose one form of expression and hold to it. The right-hand version shows that the writer has at least made his choice and abided by it.
287	
288	By this principle, an article or a preposition applying to all the members of a series must either be used only before the first term or else be repeated before each term.
289	
290	| Original | Revision |
291	| --- | --- |
292	| The French, the Italians, Spanish, and Portuguese | The French, the Italians, the Spanish, and the Portuguese |
293	| In spring, summer, or in winter | In spring, summer, or winter (In spring, in summer, or in winter) |
294	
295	Correlative expressions (*both, and*; *not, but*; *not only, but also*; *either, or*; *first, second, third*; and the like) should be followed by the same grammatical construction, that is, virtually, by the same part of speech. (Such combinations as “both Henry and I,” “not silk, but a cheap substitute,” are obviously within the rule.) Many violations of this rule (as the first three below) arise from faulty arrangement; others (as the last) from the use of unlike constructions.
296	
297	| Original | Revision |
298	| --- | --- |
299	| It was both a long ceremony and very tedious. | The ceremony was both long and tedious. |
300	| A time not for words, but action. | A time not for words, but for action. |
301	| Either you must grant his request or incur his ill will. | You must either grant his request or incur his ill will. |
302	| My objections are, first, the injustice of the measure; second, that it is unconstitutional. | My objections are, first, that the measure is unjust; second, that it is unconstitutional. |
303	
304	See also the third example under Rule 12 and the last under Rule 13.
305	
306	It may be asked, what if a writer needs to express a very large number of similar ideas, say twenty? Must he write twenty consecutive sentences of the same pattern? On closer examination he will probably find that the difficulty is imaginary, that his twenty ideas can be classified in groups, and that he need apply the principle only within each group. Otherwise he had best avoid difficulty by putting his statements in the form of a table.
307	
308	### Rule 16. Keep related words together.
309	
310	The position of the words in a sentence is the principal means of showing their relationship. The writer must therefore, so far as possible, bring together the words, and groups of words, that are related in thought, and keep apart those which are not so related.
311	
312	The subject of a sentence and the principal verb should not, as a rule, be separated by a phrase or clause that can be transferred to the beginning.
313	
314	| Original | Revision |
315	| --- | --- |
316	| Wordsworth, in the fifth book of The Excursion, gives a minute description of this church. | In the fifth book of The Excursion, Wordsworth gives a minute description of this church. |
317	| Cast iron, when treated in a Bessemer converter, is changed into steel. | By treatment in a Bessemer converter, cast iron is changed into steel. |
318	
319	The objection is that the interposed phrase or clause needlessly interrupts the natural order of the main clause. Usually, however, this objection does not hold when the order is interrupted only by a relative clause or by an expression in apposition. Nor does it hold in periodic sentences in which the interruption is a deliberately used means of creating suspense (see examples under Rule 18).
320	
321	The relative pronoun should come, as a rule, immediately after its antecedent.
322	
323	| Original | Revision |
324	| --- | --- |
325	| There was a look in his eye that boded mischief. | In his eye was a look that boded mischief. |
326	| He wrote three articles about his adventures in Spain, which were published in Harper's Magazine. | He published in Harper's Magazine three articles about his adventures in Spain. |
327	| This is a portrait of Benjamin Harrison, grandson of William Henry Harrison, who became President in 1889. | This is a portrait of Benjamin Harrison, grandson of William Henry Harrison. He became President in 1889. |
328	
329	If the antecedent consists of a group of words, the relative comes at the end of the group, unless this would cause ambiguity.
330	
331	The Superintendent of the Chicago Division, who
332	
333	| Original | Revision |
334	| --- | --- |
335	| A proposal to amend the Sherman Act, which has been variously judged. | A proposal, which has been variously judged, to amend the Sherman Act. |
336	| — | A proposal to amend the much-debated Sherman Act. |
337	| The grandson of William Henry Harrison, who | William Henry Harrison's grandson, who |
338	
339	A noun in apposition may come between antecedent and relative, because in such a combination no real ambiguity can arise.
340	
341	The Duke of York, his brother, who was regarded with hostility by the Whigs
342	
343	Modifiers should come, if possible, next to the word they modify. If several expressions modify the same word, they should be so arranged that no wrong relation is suggested.
344	
345	| Original | Revision |
346	| --- | --- |
347	| All the members were not present. | Not all the members were present. |
348	| He only found two mistakes. | He found only two mistakes. |
349	| Major R. E. Joyce will give a lecture on Tuesday evening in Bailey Hall, to which the public is invited, on “My Experiences in Mesopotamia” at eight P. M. | On Tuesday evening at eight P. M., Major R. E. Joyce will give in Bailey Hall a lecture on “My Experiences in Mesopotamia.” The public is invited. |
350	
351	### Rule 17. In summaries, keep to one tense.
352	
353	In summarizing the action of a drama, the writer should always use the present tense. In summarizing a poem, story, or novel, he should preferably use the present, though he may use the past if he prefers. If the summary is in the present tense, antecedent action should be expressed by the perfect; if in the past, by the past perfect.
354	
355	An unforeseen chance prevents Friar John from delivering Friar Lawrence's letter to Romeo. Meanwhile, owing to her father's arbitrary change of the day set for her wedding, Juliet has been compelled to drink the potion on Tuesday night, with the result that Balthasar informs Romeo of her supposed death before Friar Lawrence learns of the non-delivery of the letter.
356	
357	But whichever tense be used in the summary, a past tense in indirect discourse or in indirect question remains unchanged.
358	
359	The Friar confesses that it was he who married them.
360	
361	Apart from the exceptions noted, whichever tense the writer chooses, he should use throughout. Shifting from one tense to the other gives the appearance of uncertainty and irresolution (compare Rule 15).
362	
363	In presenting the statements or the thought of some one else, as in summarizing an essay or reporting a speech, the writer should avoid intercalating such expressions as “he said,” “he stated,” “the speaker added,” “the speaker then went on to say,” “the author also thinks,” or the like. He should indicate clearly at the outset, once for all, that what follows is summary, and then waste no words in repeating the notification.
364	
365	In notebooks, in newspapers, in handbooks of literature, summaries of one kind or another may be indispensable, and for children in primary schools it is a useful exercise to retell a story in their own words. But in the criticism or interpretation of literature the writer should be careful to avoid dropping into summary. He may find it necessary to devote one or two sentences to indicating the subject, or the opening situation, of the work he is discussing; he may cite numerous details to illustrate its qualities. But he should aim to write an orderly discussion supported by evidence, not a summary with occasional comment. Similarly, if the scope of his discussion includes a number of works, he will as a rule do better not to take them up singly in chronological order, but to aim from the beginning at establishing general conclusions.
366	
367	### Rule 18. Place the emphatic words of a sentence at the end.
368	
369	The proper place in the sentence for the word, or group of words, which the writer desires to make most prominent is usually the end.
370	
371	| Original | Revision |
372	| --- | --- |
373	| Humanity has hardly advanced in fortitude since that time, though it has advanced in many other ways. | Humanity, since that time, has advanced in many other ways, but it has hardly advanced in fortitude. |
374	| This steel is principally used for making razors, because of its hardness. | Because of its hardness, this steel is principally used in making razors. |
375	
376	The word or group of words entitled to this position of prominence is usually the logical predicate, that is, the *new* element in the sentence, as it is in the second example.
377	
378	The effectiveness of the periodic sentence arises from the prominence which it gives to the main statement.
379	
380	Four centuries ago, Christopher Columbus, one of the Italian mariners whom the decline of their own republics had put at the service of the world and of adventure, seeking for Spain a westward passage to the Indies as a set-off against the achievements of Portuguese discoverers, lighted on America.
381	
382	With these hopes and in this belief I would urge you, laying aside all hindrance, thrusting away all private aims, to devote yourself unswervingly and unflinchingly to the vigorous and successful prosecution of this war.
383	
384	The other prominent position in the sentence is the beginning. Any element in the sentence, other than the subject, may become emphatic when placed first.
385	
386	Deceit or treachery he could never forgive.
387	
388	So vast and rude, fretted by the action of nearly three thousand years, the fragments of this architecture may often seem, at first sight, like works of nature.
389	
390	A subject coming first in its sentence may be emphatic, but hardly by its position alone. In the sentence,
391	
392	Great kings worshipped at his shrine,
393	
394	the emphasis upon *kings* arises largely from its meaning and from the context. To receive special emphasis, the subject of a sentence must take the position of the predicate.
395	
396	Through the middle of the valley flowed a winding stream.
397	
398	The principle that the proper place for what is to be made most prominent is the end applies equally to the words of a sentence, to the sentences of a paragraph, and to the paragraphs of a composition.
399
