1 # Asynchronous prompt updates: what Oh My Posh needs from Nushell 2 3 Oh My Posh has a feature called [streaming](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/configuration/streaming): the prompt renders 4 immediately, slow segments (git status in a large repo, a network call, a language version lookup) show a placeholder, 5 and the prompt updates in place as each segment resolves. It works today in PowerShell, zsh, fish, and cmd (Clink). 6 We would like to support it in Nushell, but the integration requires two capabilities the Nushell REPL currently does 7 not expose. This document explains how the feature works, how the current Nushell integration is structured, and what 8 we would need from Nushell (and reedline) to make it work. 9 10 ## What streaming does 11 12 Users enable it with a single config property, the per-segment time budget in milliseconds: 13 14 ```json 15 { 16 "streaming": 100 17 } 18 ``` 19 20 With this set, Oh My Posh renders the prompt as follows: 21 22 1. Every segment starts executing concurrently, each with a 100ms budget. 23 2. Segments that finish in time render normally. Segments that exceed the budget render their `placeholder` 24 (default `...`) and keep executing in the background. 25 3. The full prompt, placeholders included, is emitted immediately. The user can start typing right away. 26 4. Each time a pending segment completes, the full prompt is re-rendered and emitted again. 27 5. The shell repaints the prompt in place with each update, without disturbing what the user has typed. 28 29 The same setting also enables a long-lived `oh-my-posh serve` daemon per shell session. The daemon renders every 30 prompt on request, which removes the process spawn per prompt (the largest prompt cost on Windows) and keeps segment 31 caches warm in memory. Both mechanisms emit the same record format; they differ only in transport and lifetime. 32 33 ## The division of labor 34 35 Oh My Posh does all the heavy lifting. The shell integration is deliberately thin. 36 37 **Oh My Posh handles:** segment execution and timeouts, rendering complete prompt strings (never diffs or partial 38 updates), caching, and process lifetime (the stream process exits on its own once every segment has resolved). 39 40 **The shell must provide four things:** 41 42 1. **Spawn and read:** start the renderer (the daemon at init, or a `stream` process per prompt) in the background and 43 read its stdout without blocking the REPL. 44 2. **Request per prompt (daemon mode):** send the daemon a render request with fresh context when a new prompt is due. 45 3. **Repaint on data:** when a new record arrives while the user sits at the prompt, replace the displayed prompt with 46 the new string and repaint, preserving the edit buffer and cursor position. 47 4. **Stop on accept:** when the user presses Enter (or Ctrl-C), stop consuming updates so a late record does not paint 48 over command output (kill the `stream` process, or send the daemon an `abort`). 49 50 Requirement 3 is the crux, and it is the one Nushell currently has no mechanism for. 51 52 ### The wire protocol 53 54 Both mechanisms write null-byte (`\0`) delimited records to stdout. Each record is one of: 55 56 - a complete, fully escaped primary prompt string, ready to display as-is, or 57 - a transient prompt (prefixed with a `\x1e` marker byte), which the shell caches so that rendering the transient 58 prompt on Enter needs no extra process spawn. 59 60 The first record arrives immediately (it is the prompt with placeholders) and is consumed synchronously, so the prompt 61 shows without delay. Subsequent records arrive as segments resolve. 62 63 **Per-prompt stream.** `oh-my-posh stream --shell=nu --status=... --execution-time=... ...` receives its context as 64 CLI flags, streams records for one prompt cycle, and exits once every segment has resolved. A fast config produces a 65 single record and exits at once. 66 67 **Session daemon.** `oh-my-posh serve --shell=nu` starts once at shell init and lives for the session. Each prompt, 68 the shell sends one JSON line, either to the daemon's stdin (zsh feeds it through a coprocess, PowerShell through the 69 process object's stdin) or to a named pipe (`--request-pipe`, used by fish): 70 71 ```json 72 {"command":"render","id":42,"status":0,"execution-time":312,"pwd":"...","terminal-width":120,"env":{"...":"..."}} 73 ``` 74 75 Records come back on stdout in the same format as `stream`, except each payload is prefixed with the request id and a 76 `\x1f` separator (`42\x1f\0`) so the shell can discard records from a superseded cycle. Two more commands 77 complete the protocol: `abort` cancels the in-flight render cycle (sent on Enter), and `quit` shuts the daemon down 78 (sent on shell exit; losing its stdout pipe also kills it, so a crashed shell never leaks a daemon). 79 80 When streaming is enabled, the generated init scripts prefer the daemon, fall back to a per-prompt `stream` process 81 when it fails (the daemon is abandoned for the session after three failures), and fall back to classic synchronous 82 rendering when both are unavailable. 83 84 ## How the four supported shells do it 85 86 Each shell uses whatever async primitive, daemon transport, and repaint API it has: 87 88 | Shell | Async notification | Daemon requests via | Repaint call | 89 | ---------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | 90 | zsh | `zle -F handler` (fd watcher inside the line editor) | coprocess stdin | set `PS1`, then `zle reset-prompt` | 91 | fish | background reader process signals the shell with `SIGUSR1` | named pipe (`--request-pipe`) | set prompt variable, then `commandline -f repaint` | 92 | PowerShell | `PowerShell.OnIdle` engine event drains a shared queue | process object stdin | `[Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()` | 93 | cmd | Clink Lua coroutines poll the daemon | daemon stdin | `clink.refilterprompt()` | 94 95 In every case the repaint API redraws the prompt while keeping the user's partially typed command line intact. That 96 API is the load-bearing piece. 97 98 ### Sequence flow: per-prompt stream (zsh as the reference) 99 100 ```mermaid 101 sequenceDiagram 102 participant U as User 103 participant S as Shell (line editor) 104 participant O as oh-my-posh stream 105 106 S->>O: spawn with context (status, duration, pwd, ...) 107 O-->>S: record 1: prompt with "..." placeholders 108 Note over S: blocking read, ~instant 109 S->>U: display prompt 110 U->>S: starts typing 111 Note over O: git segment resolves after 400ms 112 O-->>S: record 2: full prompt, git segment filled in 113 Note over S: fd watcher fires (zle -F) 114 S->>S: PS1 = record 2, zle reset-prompt 115 Note over U: prompt updates in place,
typed input untouched 116 O-->>S: record 3 (\x1e prefix): transient prompt 117 S->>S: cache transient prompt 118 Note over O: all segments resolved 119 O-->>S: EOF, process exits 120 U->>S: presses Enter 121 S->>S: close fd (stops any in-flight stream),
print cached transient prompt 122 S->>U: command executes 123 ``` 124 125 ### Sequence flow: session daemon 126 127 The daemon path is the same cycle without the spawn, repeated for every prompt: 128 129 ```mermaid 130 sequenceDiagram 131 participant U as User 132 participant S as Shell (line editor) 133 participant D as oh-my-posh serve 134 135 Note over S,D: daemon started once at shell init 136 Note over S: previous command finished,
new prompt due 137 S->>D: {"command":"render","id":42,...}
(stdin or named pipe) 138 D-->>S: 42\x1f\0 139 S->>U: display prompt 140 U->>S: starts typing 141 D-->>S: 42\x1f\0 142 S->>S: id matches current cycle: repaint in place 143 D-->>S: 42\x1f\x1e\0 144 S->>S: cache transient prompt 145 U->>S: presses Enter 146 S->>D: {"command":"abort"} 147 S->>U: cached transient prompt, command executes 148 Note over S,D: daemon stays alive for the next prompt 149 ``` 150 151 ## Where this touches the current Nushell integration 152 153 Today's init script (generated by `oh-my-posh init nu`) is fully synchronous. The relevant part: 154 155 ```nu 156 $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| 157 # gather context (cleared screen detection, SET_POSHCONTEXT) ... 158 _omp_get_prompt primary $"--cleared=($clear)" 159 } 160 161 $env.PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT = {|| _omp_get_prompt right } 162 ``` 163 164 `_omp_get_prompt` calls `^oh-my-posh print primary ...` and blocks until every segment has finished. A slow git 165 repository holds the entire prompt hostage. This closure is exactly where the streaming path would slot in: send the 166 daemon a render request (or spawn `oh-my-posh stream` as the fallback), return the first record, and let later records 167 repaint. The daemon itself would start once, during init, next to where the script currently renders 168 `$env.PROMPT_MULTILINE_INDICATOR`. 169 170 The rest of the feature already maps cleanly onto existing Nushell hooks: 171 172 - **Transient prompt** works today via `$env.TRANSIENT_PROMPT_COMMAND`; the streamed transient record would only make 173 it cheaper (serve it from cache instead of spawning a process on every Enter). 174 - **Stop on accept** (requirement 4) maps onto the existing `pre_execution` hook: send the daemon an `abort`, or kill 175 the fallback stream process. 176 - **Daemon shutdown** needs no hook at all: the daemon exits when its stdout pipe closes, so it dies with the shell 177 even when the shell never gets to say `quit`. 178 179 ## What Nushell is missing 180 181 Nushell evaluates `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` once per prompt cycle, converts the result to a string, and hands it to 182 reedline as static prompt content before `read_line()` starts. From that point until the line is submitted, two things 183 are impossible: 184 185 1. **Nothing external can wake the REPL.** `job spawn` gives us a background thread that could read the stream, and 186 `job send 0` can queue a message for the main thread, but the main thread is parked inside `read_line()` and only 187 processes key events. There is no fd watcher, no signal handler, no timer, and no job-message delivery that fires 188 while the editor waits for input. (`SIGUSR1`, which the fish integration relies on, would not fit Nushell's 189 cross-platform model anyway; a channel-based wake fits better.) 190 2. **Prompt content cannot change mid-edit.** Even if a repaint happens (reedline has `ReedlineEvent::Repaint`, though 191 it is only reachable through a keybinding), reedline re-queries the `Prompt` trait, and Nushell hands back the 192 same stored string. There is no way to say "the prompt is now this new string, redraw it" while the 193 buffer stays intact. 194 195 Reedline already contains most of the required plumbing in another feature: the `ExternalPrinter` is a 196 channel that background threads write to, which reedline polls during `read_line()` and which triggers a safe redraw 197 (print above the prompt, restore the buffer). Asynchronous prompt updates need the same wake-and-redraw cycle with one 198 difference: the payload replaces the prompt instead of printing above it. 199 200 ## The ask 201 202 A mechanism for a background job to replace the prompt while `read_line()` is active, preserving the edit buffer and 203 cursor. Two shapes would work for us, in order of preference: 204 205 ### Option A: push-based prompt updates (preferred) 206 207 Expose a way for a background job to hand the REPL a pre-rendered prompt string and trigger a repaint, such as a 208 `commandline` subcommand: 209 210 ```nu 211 commandline set-prompt --left $new_prompt # swap content + repaint, buffer untouched 212 ``` 213 214 Internally this could mirror the `ExternalPrinter` design: a bounded channel created by the host, polled in 215 reedline's `read_line()` event loop; on receipt, update the prompt content held by the host's `Prompt` object 216 and repaint. Oh My Posh sends complete prompt strings, so nothing needs re-evaluation, and this stays independent of 217 `PROMPT_COMMAND` semantics. 218 219 ### Option B: externally triggered prompt re-evaluation 220 221 A way for a background job to request a full prompt refresh, where Nushell re-runs `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` (and 222 `PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT`) and repaints in place. This also works, but costs a closure evaluation per update and has 223 trickier semantics (the closure runs on the engine while a command line is being edited). 224 225 ### What the resulting integration would look like 226 227 With Option A, the streaming path in `omp.nu` would be roughly: 228 229 ```nu 230 $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| 231 # spawn the stream; read records in the background 232 job spawn { 233 ^$_omp_executable stream --shell=nu ...context... 234 | split row (char --integer 0) # \0 delimited records 235 | each {|record| 236 if ($record | str starts-with (char --integer 30)) { 237 # \x1e prefix: cache the transient prompt for later 238 $env._omp_transient = ($record | str substring 1..) 239 } else { 240 commandline set-prompt --left $record # <- the missing capability 241 } 242 } 243 } 244 245 # ... return the first record synchronously so the prompt shows instantly 246 } 247 ``` 248 249 (Exact plumbing aside: everything except `commandline set-prompt` is expressible in Nushell today.) 250 251 The daemon variant has the same shape. The job holds `oh-my-posh serve` for the whole session instead of one `stream` 252 process per prompt, `PROMPT_COMMAND` writes a JSON render request to it (the named-pipe transport the fish integration 253 uses exists precisely because fish cannot write to a running process's stdin; Nushell could use the same flag), and 254 the reader discards records whose id is not the current cycle's. The missing capability is identical. 255 256 ### Sequence flow of the proposed integration 257 258 ```mermaid 259 sequenceDiagram 260 participant U as User 261 participant R as reedline (read_line) 262 participant N as Nushell engine 263 participant J as background job 264 participant O as oh-my-posh stream 265 266 N->>N: PROMPT_COMMAND runs 267 N->>O: spawn stream 268 O-->>N: record 1 (placeholders) 269 N->>J: job spawn (reader) 270 N->>R: read_line(prompt = record 1) 271 R->>U: display prompt 272 U->>R: starts typing 273 O-->>J: record 2 (segment resolved) 274 J->>R: prompt-update channel: record 2 275 Note over R: poll wakes, swap prompt, repaint,
buffer and cursor preserved 276 R->>U: updated prompt, input intact 277 U->>R: presses Enter 278 R->>N: Signal::Success 279 N->>J: pre_execution hook: job kill (stops stream) 280 ``` 281 282 ## Guarantees from our side 283 284 To keep the shell-side risk low, the Oh My Posh contract is strict: 285 286 - Every record is a complete prompt string, already escaped for the target shell. The shell never assembles or diffs 287 anything. 288 - Records are strictly newer-wins; applying only the latest received record is always correct, so a slow consumer can 289 coalesce updates. In daemon mode the id prefix also lets the shell drop records from a superseded cycle. 290 - The `stream` process exits on its own once all segments resolve; both it and the daemon die on SIGPIPE/EOF when the 291 shell goes away, so there is nothing to reap in the happy path. 292 - The integration degrades gracefully: daemon failure falls back to a per-prompt `stream` process (the daemon is 293 abandoned for the session after repeated failures), and if that fails too, to today's synchronous rendering. Users 294 without `streaming` in their config keep the exact current behavior. 295 296 We are happy to build and maintain the Nushell side of the integration (as we do for the other shells) and to adjust 297 our protocol if a different API shape lands. The single capability that unblocks this, and likely every other 298 async-prompt tool in the ecosystem (starship and friends have the same limitation), is: **let a background job replace 299 the prompt and repaint while the line editor is active.** 300

input.new_string:
We would like to support it in Nushell, but Nushell has no way to update the prompt once the line editor is active.

It's a variant of the `ReedlineEvent` enum in the reedline crate (nushell/reedline, defined in `src/enums.rs`) — the set of high-level editor actions that keybindings dispatch. When reedline's engine loop processes a `Repaint`, it calls the painter to redraw the prompt and the edit buffer, re-querying the `Prompt` trait object for the prompt strings as it does.

Two facts about it that the document relies on, both now verified:

1. **It's key-triggered only.** Nothing inside reedline fires it asynchronously (resize has its own `Resize` event). The only way to reach it is a keypress bound to it — and Nushell does expose that binding: I just ran `keybindings list --events` on your local nu, and `Repaint` is in the list, so a user can put `event: { send: repaint }` in `$env.config.keybindings` today.

2. **It redraws stale content.** Even when it fires, Nushell's `Prompt` implementation hands back the strings it stored before `read_line()` started (the `PROMPT_COMMAND` result), so the repaint reproduces the same prompt.

That combination is exactly why it's worth citing in the issue: the safe mid-edit redraw machinery already exists and works — what's missing is a non-keyboard trigger and fresh content for it to draw. It also gives the Nushell maintainers a one-line experiment to see the gap: bind a key to `repaint`, run a slow prompt, press the key — the prompt redraws, unchanged.

One correction this surfaced for the document: the parenthetical currently says `Repaint` "is only reachable through a keybinding" — accurate, but I could strengthen it by noting Nushell already exposes that binding in `$env.config.keybindings`, which makes the "plumbing exists, trigger is missing" argument more concrete. 1 # Asynchronous prompt updates: what Oh My Posh needs from Nushell 2 3 Oh My Posh has a feature called [streaming](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/configuration/streaming): the prompt renders 4 immediately, slow segments (git status in a large repo, a network call, a language version lookup) show a placeholder, 5 and the prompt updates in place as each segment resolves. It works today in PowerShell, zsh, fish, and cmd (Clink). 6 We would like to support it in Nushell, but Nushell has no way to update the prompt once the line editor is active. 7 This document explains how the feature works, where it touches the current Nushell integration, and what we need from 8 Nushell (and reedline). 9 10 ## What streaming does 11 12 Users enable it with a single config property, the per-segment time budget in milliseconds: 13 14 ```json 15 { 16 "streaming": 100 17 } 18 ``` 19 20 With this set, Oh My Posh renders the prompt as follows: 21 22 1. All segments execute concurrently, each with a 100ms budget. 23 2. Segments that finish in time render normally. Segments that exceed the budget render their `placeholder` 24 (default `...`) and keep executing in the background. 25 3. Oh My Posh emits the full prompt, placeholders included, immediately. The user can start typing right away. 26 4. Each time a pending segment completes, Oh My Posh re-renders the full prompt and emits it again. 27 5. The shell repaints the prompt in place with each update, without disturbing what the user has typed. 28 29 The same setting also enables a long-lived `oh-my-posh serve` daemon per shell session. The daemon renders every 30 prompt on request, which removes the process spawn per prompt (the largest prompt cost on Windows) and keeps segment 31 caches warm in memory. Both mechanisms emit the same record format; they differ only in transport and lifetime. 32 33 ## The division of labor 34 35 Oh My Posh does all the heavy lifting. The shell integration is deliberately thin. 36 37 **Oh My Posh handles:** segment execution and timeouts, rendering complete prompt strings (never diffs or partial 38 updates), caching, and process lifetime (the stream process exits on its own once every segment has resolved). 39 40 **The shell must provide four things:** 41 42 1. **Spawn and read:** start the renderer (the daemon at init, or a `stream` process per prompt) in the background and 43 read its stdout without blocking the REPL. 44 2. **Request per prompt (daemon mode):** send the daemon a render request with fresh context when a new prompt is due. 45 3. **Repaint on data:** when a new record arrives while the user sits at the prompt, replace the displayed prompt with 46 the new string and repaint, preserving the edit buffer and cursor position. 47 4. **Stop on accept:** when the user presses Enter (or Ctrl-C), stop consuming updates so a late record does not paint 48 over command output (kill the `stream` process, or send the daemon an `abort`). 49 50 Requirement 3 is the crux, and the one Nushell lacks. 51 52 ### The wire protocol 53 54 Both mechanisms write null-byte (`\0`) delimited records to stdout. Each record is one of: 55 56 - a complete, fully escaped primary prompt string, ready to display as-is, or 57 - a transient prompt (prefixed with a `\x1e` marker byte), which the shell caches so that rendering the transient 58 prompt on Enter needs no extra process spawn. 59 60 The shell consumes the first record (the prompt with placeholders) synchronously, so the prompt shows without delay. 61 Later records arrive as segments resolve. 62 63 **Per-prompt stream.** `oh-my-posh stream --shell=nu --status=... --execution-time=... ...` receives its context as 64 CLI flags, streams records for one prompt cycle, and exits once every segment has resolved. A fast config produces a 65 single record and exits at once. 66 67 **Session daemon.** `oh-my-posh serve --shell=nu` starts once at shell init and lives for the session. Each prompt, 68 the shell sends one JSON line, either to the daemon's stdin (zsh feeds it through a coprocess, PowerShell through the 69 process object's stdin) or to a named pipe (`--request-pipe`, used by fish): 70 71 ```json 72 {"command":"render","id":42,"status":0,"execution-time":312,"pwd":"...","terminal-width":120,"env":{"...":"..."}} 73 ``` 74 75 Records come back on stdout in the same format as `stream`, except each payload is prefixed with the request id and a 76 `\x1f` separator (`42\x1f\0`) so the shell can discard records from a superseded cycle. Two more commands 77 complete the protocol: `abort` cancels the in-flight render cycle (sent on Enter), and `quit` shuts the daemon down 78 (sent on shell exit; losing its stdout pipe also kills it, so a crashed shell never leaks a daemon). 79 80 The generated init scripts prefer the daemon, fall back to a per-prompt `stream` process when it fails (after three 81 failures the script abandons the daemon for the session), and fall back to classic synchronous rendering when both 82 are unavailable. 83 84 ## How the four supported shells do it 85 86 Each shell uses whatever async primitive, daemon transport, and repaint API it has: 87 88 | Shell | Async notification | Daemon requests via | Repaint call | 89 | ---------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | 90 | zsh | `zle -F handler` (fd watcher inside the line editor) | coprocess stdin | set `PS1`, then `zle reset-prompt` | 91 | fish | background reader process signals the shell with `SIGUSR1` | named pipe (`--request-pipe`) | set prompt variable, then `commandline -f repaint` | 92 | PowerShell | `PowerShell.OnIdle` engine event drains a shared queue | process object stdin | `[Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()` | 93 | cmd | Clink Lua coroutines poll the daemon | daemon stdin | `clink.refilterprompt()` | 94 95 In every case the repaint API redraws the prompt while keeping the user's partially typed command line intact. That 96 API is the load-bearing piece. 97 98 ### Sequence flow: per-prompt stream (zsh as the reference) 99 100 ```mermaid 101 sequenceDiagram 102 participant U as User 103 participant S as Shell (line editor) 104 participant O as oh-my-posh stream 105 106 S->>O: spawn with context (status, duration, pwd, ...) 107 O-->>S: record 1: prompt with "..." placeholders 108 Note over S: blocking read, ~instant 109 S->>U: display prompt 110 U->>S: starts typing 111 Note over O: git segment resolves after 400ms 112 O-->>S: record 2: full prompt, git segment filled in 113 Note over S: fd watcher fires (zle -F) 114 S->>S: PS1 = record 2, zle reset-prompt 115 Note over U: prompt updates in place,
typed input untouched 116 O-->>S: record 3 (\x1e prefix): transient prompt 117 S->>S: cache transient prompt 118 Note over O: all segments resolved 119 O-->>S: EOF, process exits 120 U->>S: presses Enter 121 S->>S: close fd (stops any in-flight stream),
print cached transient prompt 122 S->>U: command executes 123 ``` 124 125 ### Sequence flow: session daemon 126 127 The daemon path is the same cycle without the spawn, repeated for every prompt: 128 129 ```mermaid 130 sequenceDiagram 131 participant U as User 132 participant S as Shell (line editor) 133 participant D as oh-my-posh serve 134 135 Note over S,D: daemon started once at shell init 136 Note over S: previous command finished,
new prompt due 137 S->>D: {"command":"render","id":42,...}
(stdin or named pipe) 138 D-->>S: 42\x1f\0 139 S->>U: display prompt 140 U->>S: starts typing 141 D-->>S: 42\x1f\0 142 S->>S: id matches current cycle: repaint in place 143 D-->>S: 42\x1f\x1e\0 144 S->>S: cache transient prompt 145 U->>S: presses Enter 146 S->>D: {"command":"abort"} 147 S->>U: cached transient prompt, command executes 148 Note over S,D: daemon stays alive for the next prompt 149 ``` 150 151 ## Where this touches the current Nushell integration 152 153 Today's init script (generated by `oh-my-posh init nu`) is fully synchronous. The relevant part: 154 155 ```nu 156 $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| 157 # gather context (cleared screen detection, SET_POSHCONTEXT) ... 158 _omp_get_prompt primary $"--cleared=($clear)" 159 } 160 161 $env.PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT = {|| _omp_get_prompt right } 162 ``` 163 164 `_omp_get_prompt` calls `^oh-my-posh print primary ...` and blocks until every segment has finished. A slow git 165 repository holds the entire prompt hostage. This closure is where the streaming path slots in: send the daemon a 166 render request (or spawn `oh-my-posh stream` as the fallback), return the first record, and let later records repaint. 167 The daemon itself would start once, during init, next to where the script renders 168 `$env.PROMPT_MULTILINE_INDICATOR`. 169 170 The rest of the feature maps onto existing Nushell hooks: 171 172 - **Transient prompt** works today via `$env.TRANSIENT_PROMPT_COMMAND`; the streamed transient record would only make 173 it cheaper (serve it from cache instead of spawning a process on every Enter). 174 - **Stop on accept** (requirement 4) maps onto the existing `pre_execution` hook: send the daemon an `abort`, or kill 175 the fallback stream process. 176 - **Daemon shutdown** needs no hook at all: the daemon exits when its stdout pipe closes, so it dies with the shell 177 even when the shell never gets to say `quit`. 178 179 ## What Nushell is missing 180 181 Nushell evaluates `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` once per prompt cycle, converts the result to a string, and hands it to 182 reedline as static prompt content before `read_line()` starts. From that point until the line is submitted, two things 183 are impossible: 184 185 1. **Nothing external can wake the REPL.** `job spawn` gives us a background thread that could read the stream, and 186 `job send 0` can queue a message for the main thread, but the main thread is parked inside `read_line()` and only 187 processes key events. There is no fd watcher, no signal handler, no timer, and no job-message delivery that fires 188 while the editor waits for input. (`SIGUSR1`, which the fish integration relies on, would not fit Nushell's 189 cross-platform model anyway; a channel-based wake fits better.) 190 2. **Prompt content cannot change mid-edit.** Even if a repaint happens (reedline has `ReedlineEvent::Repaint`, though 191 it is only reachable through a keybinding), reedline re-queries the `Prompt` trait, and Nushell hands back the 192 same stored string. There is no way to say "the prompt is now this new string, redraw it" while the 193 buffer stays intact. 194 195 Reedline already contains most of the required plumbing in another feature: the `ExternalPrinter` is a 196 channel that background threads write to, which reedline polls during `read_line()` and which triggers a safe redraw 197 (print above the prompt, restore the buffer). Asynchronous prompt updates need the same wake-and-redraw cycle with one 198 difference: the payload replaces the prompt instead of printing above it. 199 200 ## The ask 201 202 A mechanism for a background job to replace the prompt while `read_line()` is active, preserving the edit buffer and 203 cursor. Two shapes would work for us, in order of preference: 204 205 ### Option A: push-based prompt updates (preferred) 206 207 Expose a way for a background job to hand the REPL a pre-rendered prompt string and trigger a repaint, such as a 208 `commandline` subcommand: 209 210 ```nu 211 commandline set-prompt --left $new_prompt # swap content + repaint, buffer untouched 212 ``` 213 214 Internally this could mirror the `ExternalPrinter` design: a bounded channel created by the host, polled in 215 reedline's `read_line()` event loop; on receipt, update the prompt content held by the host's `Prompt` object 216 and repaint. Oh My Posh sends complete prompt strings, so nothing needs re-evaluation, and this stays independent of 217 `PROMPT_COMMAND` semantics. 218 219 ### Option B: externally triggered prompt re-evaluation 220 221 A way for a background job to request a full prompt refresh, where Nushell re-runs `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` (and 222 `PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT`) and repaints in place. This also works, but costs a closure evaluation per update and has 223 trickier semantics (the closure runs on the engine while a command line is being edited). 224 225 ### What the resulting integration would look like 226 227 With Option A, the streaming path in `omp.nu` would be roughly: 228 229 ```nu 230 $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| 231 # spawn the stream; read records in the background 232 job spawn { 233 ^$_omp_executable stream --shell=nu ...context... 234 | split row (char --integer 0) # \0 delimited records 235 | each {|record| 236 if ($record | str starts-with (char --integer 30)) { 237 # \x1e prefix: cache the transient prompt for later 238 $env._omp_transient = ($record | str substring 1..) 239 } else { 240 commandline set-prompt --left $record # <- the missing capability 241 } 242 } 243 } 244 245 # ... return the first record synchronously so the prompt shows instantly 246 } 247 ``` 248 249 (Exact plumbing aside: everything except `commandline set-prompt` is expressible in Nushell today.) 250 251 The daemon variant has the same shape: the job holds `oh-my-posh serve` for the session instead of one `stream` 252 process per prompt, `PROMPT_COMMAND` writes a JSON render request to it, and the reader discards records from stale 253 cycles. The named-pipe transport exists precisely because fish cannot write to a running process's stdin either; 254 Nushell could reuse that flag. It works today in PowerShell, zsh, fish, and cmd (Clink). We would like to support it in Nushell, but Nushell has no way to update the prompt once the line editor is active. This document explains how the feature works, where it touches the current Nushell integration, and what we need from Nushell (and reedline). ## What streaming does Users enable it with a single config property, the per-segment time budget in milliseconds: ```json { "streaming": 100 } ``` With this set, Oh My Posh renders the prompt as follows: 1. All segments execute concurrently, each with a 100ms budget. 2. Segments that finish in time render normally. Segments that exceed the budget render their `placeholder` (default `...`) and keep executing in the background. 3. Oh My Posh emits the full prompt, placeholders included, immediately. The user can start typing right away. 4. Each time a pending segment completes, Oh My Posh re-renders the full prompt and emits it again. 5. The shell repaints the prompt in place with each update, without disturbing what the user has typed. The same setting also enables a long-lived `oh-my-posh serve` daemon per shell session. The daemon renders every prompt on request, which removes the process spawn per prompt (the largest prompt cost on Windows) and keeps segment caches warm in memory. Both mechanisms emit the same record format; they differ only in transport and lifetime. ## The division of labor Oh My Posh does all the heavy lifting. The shell integration is deliberately thin. **Oh My Posh handles:** segment execution and timeouts, rendering complete prompt strings (never diffs or partial updates), caching, and process lifetime (the stream process exits on its own once every segment has resolved). **The shell must provide four things:** 1. **Spawn and read:** start the renderer (the daemon at init, or a `stream` process per prompt) in the background and read its stdout without blocking the REPL. 2. **Request per prompt (daemon mode):** send the daemon a render request with fresh context when a new prompt is due. 3. **Repaint on data:** when a new record arrives while the user sits at the prompt, replace the displayed prompt with the new string and repaint, preserving the edit buffer and cursor position. 4. **Stop on accept:** when the user presses Enter (or Ctrl-C), stop consuming updates so a late record does not paint over command output (kill the `stream` process, or send the daemon an `abort`). Requirement 3 is the crux, and the one Nushell lacks. ### The wire protocol Both mechanisms write null-byte (`\0`) delimited records to stdout. Each record is one of: - a complete, fully escaped primary prompt string, ready to display as-is, or - a transient prompt (prefixed with a `\x1e` marker byte), which the shell caches so that rendering the transient prompt on Enter needs no extra process spawn. The shell consumes the first record (the prompt with placeholders) synchronously, so the prompt shows without delay. Later records arrive as segments resolve. **Per-prompt stream.** `oh-my-posh stream --shell=nu --status=... --execution-time=... ...` receives its context as CLI flags, streams records for one prompt cycle, and exits once every segment has resolved. A fast config produces a single record and exits at once. **Session daemon.** `oh-my-posh serve --shell=nu` starts once at shell init and lives for the session. Each prompt, the shell sends one JSON line, either to the daemon's stdin (zsh feeds it through a coprocess, PowerShell through the process object's stdin) or to a named pipe (`--request-pipe`, used by fish): ```json {"command":"render","id":42,"status":0,"execution-time":312,"pwd":"...","terminal-width":120,"env":{"...":"..."}} ``` Records come back on stdout in the same format as `stream`, except each payload is prefixed with the request id and a `\x1f` separator (`42\x1f\0`) so the shell can discard records from a superseded cycle. Two more commands complete the protocol: `abort` cancels the in-flight render cycle (sent on Enter), and `quit` shuts the daemon down (sent on shell exit; losing its stdout pipe also kills it, so a crashed shell never leaks a daemon). The generated init scripts prefer the daemon, fall back to a per-prompt `stream` process when it fails (after three failures the script abandons the daemon for the session), and fall back to classic synchronous rendering when both are unavailable. ## How the four supported shells do it Each shell uses whatever async primitive, daemon transport, and repaint API it has: | Shell | Async notification | Daemon requests via | Repaint call | | ---------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- | | zsh | `zle -F handler` (fd watcher inside the line editor) | coprocess stdin | set `PS1`, then `zle reset-prompt` | | fish | background reader process signals the shell with `SIGUSR1` | named pipe (`--request-pipe`) | set prompt variable, then `commandline -f repaint` | | PowerShell | `PowerShell.OnIdle` engine event drains a shared queue | process object stdin | `[Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()` | | cmd | Clink Lua coroutines poll the daemon | daemon stdin | `clink.refilterprompt()` | In every case the repaint API redraws the prompt while keeping the user's partially typed command line intact. That API is the load-bearing piece. ### Sequence flow: per-prompt stream (zsh as the reference) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant S as Shell (line editor) participant O as oh-my-posh stream S->>O: spawn with context (status, duration, pwd, ...) O-->>S: record 1: prompt with "..." placeholders Note over S: blocking read, ~instant S->>U: display prompt U->>S: starts typing Note over O: git segment resolves after 400ms O-->>S: record 2: full prompt, git segment filled in Note over S: fd watcher fires (zle -F) S->>S: PS1 = record 2, zle reset-prompt Note over U: prompt updates in place,
typed input untouched O-->>S: record 3 (\x1e prefix): transient prompt S->>S: cache transient prompt Note over O: all segments resolved O-->>S: EOF, process exits U->>S: presses Enter S->>S: close fd (stops any in-flight stream),
print cached transient prompt S->>U: command executes ``` ### Sequence flow: session daemon The daemon path is the same cycle without the spawn, repeated for every prompt: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant S as Shell (line editor) participant D as oh-my-posh serve Note over S,D: daemon started once at shell init Note over S: previous command finished,
new prompt due S->>D: {"command":"render","id":42,...}
(stdin or named pipe) D-->>S: 42\x1f\0 S->>U: display prompt U->>S: starts typing D-->>S: 42\x1f\0 S->>S: id matches current cycle: repaint in place D-->>S: 42\x1f\x1e\0 S->>S: cache transient prompt U->>S: presses Enter S->>D: {"command":"abort"} S->>U: cached transient prompt, command executes Note over S,D: daemon stays alive for the next prompt ``` ## Where this touches the current Nushell integration Today's init script (generated by `oh-my-posh init nu`) is fully synchronous. The relevant part: ```nu $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| # gather context (cleared screen detection, SET_POSHCONTEXT) ... _omp_get_prompt primary $"--cleared=($clear)" } $env.PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT = {|| _omp_get_prompt right } ``` `_omp_get_prompt` calls `^oh-my-posh print primary ...` and blocks until every segment has finished. A slow git repository holds the entire prompt hostage. This closure is where the streaming path slots in: send the daemon a render request (or spawn `oh-my-posh stream` as the fallback), return the first record, and let later records repaint. The daemon itself would start once, during init, next to where the script renders `$env.PROMPT_MULTILINE_INDICATOR`. The rest of the feature maps onto existing Nushell hooks: - **Transient prompt** works today via `$env.TRANSIENT_PROMPT_COMMAND`; the streamed transient record would only make it cheaper (serve it from cache instead of spawning a process on every Enter). - **Stop on accept** (requirement 4) maps onto the existing `pre_execution` hook: send the daemon an `abort`, or kill the fallback stream process. - **Daemon shutdown** needs no hook at all: the daemon exits when its stdout pipe closes, so it dies with the shell even when the shell never gets to say `quit`. ## What Nushell is missing Nushell evaluates `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` once per prompt cycle, converts the result to a string, and hands it to reedline as static prompt content before `read_line()` starts. From that point until the line is submitted, two things are impossible: 1. **Nothing external can wake the REPL.** `job spawn` gives us a background thread that could read the stream, and `job send 0` can queue a message for the main thread, but the main thread is parked inside `read_line()` and only processes key events. There is no fd watcher, no signal handler, no timer, and no job-message delivery that fires while the editor waits for input. (`SIGUSR1`, which the fish integration relies on, would not fit Nushell's cross-platform model anyway; a channel-based wake fits better.) 2. **Prompt content cannot change mid-edit.** Reedline has `ReedlineEvent::Repaint`, and Nushell already exposes it in `$env.config.keybindings` as `event: { send: repaint }`, but only a keypress can trigger it, and when it fires, reedline re-queries the `Prompt` trait and Nushell hands back the same stored string. Binding a key to `repaint` and pressing it mid-edit demonstrates both halves of the gap: the redraw itself is safe and buffer-preserving, yet the content never changes. There is no way to say "the prompt is now this new string, redraw it". Reedline already contains most of the required plumbing in another feature: the `ExternalPrinter` is a channel that background threads write to, which reedline polls during `read_line()` and which triggers a safe redraw (print above the prompt, restore the buffer). Asynchronous prompt updates need the same wake-and-redraw cycle with one difference: the payload replaces the prompt instead of printing above it. ## The ask A mechanism for a background job to replace the prompt while `read_line()` is active, preserving the edit buffer and cursor. Two shapes would work for us, in order of preference: ### Option A: push-based prompt updates (preferred) Expose a way for a background job to hand the REPL a pre-rendered prompt string and trigger a repaint, such as a `commandline` subcommand: ```nu commandline set-prompt --left $new_prompt # swap content + repaint, buffer untouched ``` Internally this could mirror the `ExternalPrinter` design: a bounded channel created by the host, polled in reedline's `read_line()` event loop; on receipt, update the prompt content held by the host's `Prompt` object and repaint. Oh My Posh sends complete prompt strings, so nothing needs re-evaluation, and this stays independent of `PROMPT_COMMAND` semantics. ### Option B: externally triggered prompt re-evaluation A way for a background job to request a full prompt refresh, where Nushell re-runs `$env.PROMPT_COMMAND` (and `PROMPT_COMMAND_RIGHT`) and repaints in place. This also works, but costs a closure evaluation per update and has trickier semantics (the closure runs on the engine while a command line is being edited). ### What the resulting integration would look like With Option A, the streaming path in `omp.nu` would be roughly: ```nu $env.PROMPT_COMMAND = {|| # spawn the stream; read records in the background job spawn { ^$_omp_executable stream --shell=nu ...context... | split row (char --integer 0) # \0 delimited records | each {|record| if ($record | str starts-with (char --integer 30)) { # \x1e prefix: cache the transient prompt for later $env._omp_transient = ($record | str substring 1..) } else { commandline set-prompt --left $record # <- the missing capability } } } # ... return the first record synchronously so the prompt shows instantly } ``` (Exact plumbing aside: everything except `commandline set-prompt` is expressible in Nushell today.) The daemon variant has the same shape: the job holds `oh-my-posh serve` for the session instead of one `stream` process per prompt, `PROMPT_COMMAND` writes a JSON render request to it, and the reader discards records from stale cycles. The named-pipe transport exists precisely because fish cannot write to a running process's stdin either; Nushell could reuse that flag. The missing capability is identical. ### Sequence flow of the proposed integration ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant R as reedline (read_line) participant N as Nushell engine participant J as background job participant O as oh-my-posh stream N->>N: PROMPT_COMMAND runs N->>O: spawn stream O-->>N: record 1 (placeholders) N->>J: job spawn (reader) N->>R: read_line(prompt = record 1) R->>U: display prompt U->>R: starts typing O-->>J: record 2 (segment resolved) J->>R: prompt-update channel: record 2 Note over R: poll wakes, swap prompt, repaint,
buffer and cursor preserved R->>U: updated prompt, input intact U->>R: presses Enter R->>N: Signal::Success N->>J: pre_execution hook: job kill (stops stream) ``` ## Guarantees from our side To keep the shell-side risk low, the Oh My Posh contract is strict: - Every record is a complete prompt string, already escaped for the target shell. The shell never assembles or diffs anything. - Records are strictly newer-wins; applying only the latest received record is always correct, so a slow consumer can coalesce updates. In daemon mode the id prefix also lets the shell drop records from a superseded cycle. - The `stream` process exits on its own once all segments resolve; both it and the daemon die on SIGPIPE/EOF when the shell goes away, so there is nothing to reap in the happy path. - The integration degrades gracefully: daemon failure falls back to a per-prompt `stream` process, and if that fails too, to today's synchronous rendering. Users without `streaming` in their config keep the exact current behavior. We will build and maintain the Nushell side of the integration, as we do for the other shells, and adjust our protocol if a different API shape lands. The single capability that unblocks this, and likely every other async-prompt tool in the ecosystem (starship and friends share the limitation), is: **let a background job replace the prompt and repaint while the line editor is active.**

We will build and maintain the Nushell side of the integration, as we do for the other shells, and adjust our protocol if a different API shape lands. The single capability that unblocks this, and likely every other async-prompt tool in the ecosystem (starship and friends share the limitation), is: **let a background job replace the prompt and repaint while the line editor is active.**
